desc: Gjirokastër is one of the Balkans' most characterful inland heritage bases: a steep UNESCO stone city with slate-roofed Ottoman houses, a huge hilltop castle, and a more lived-in, less polished atmosphere than many western European old towns. It works best for travelers who want history, views, and Albanian character rather than a resort gloss.
hls: The stone-roof skyline gives Gjirokastër a silhouette that feels instantly different from almost anywhere else in the region.
The castle is not just a viewpoint add-on; it dominates the whole city and gives the destination real historical weight.
The old bazaar still feels like the active heart of town rather than a sealed-off heritage set piece.
Traditional tower houses such as Skenduli and Zekate make the Ottoman domestic history legible in a way many fortress towns never manage.
Summer is dry, bright, and photogenic, but the steep streets and stone surfaces make midday heat more intense than the raw averages suggest.
May-June and September usually land the best mix of touring comfort, clear light, and manageable crowd pressure.

todo.name: Gjirokastra Castle
todo.desc: Give the citadel real time, because the scale, museum areas, and valley views are the anchor of the whole destination.

todo.name: Old Bazaar wander
todo.desc: Walk the bazaar slowly instead of treating it as a pass-through, since its rebuilt uniform facades are part of what makes the old town coherent.

todo.name: Skenduli or Zekate house
todo.desc: Visit at least one tower house so Gjirokastër reads as a domestic-architecture destination, not only a castle stop.

todo.name: Obelisk viewpoint climb
todo.desc: Fit in the obelisk for the classic roofscape-and-valley panorama over the stone city.

todo.name: Upper-quarter stone street walk
todo.desc: Keep one slower block for the steep lanes between bazaar and castle, when the town feels most atmospheric.

todo.name: Traditional southern Albanian dinner
todo.desc: Use one evening for regional cooking in the old town so the trip includes more than heritage-site box-ticking.

wifi.note: Usually fine for basic remote work in central guesthouses and newer hotels, but historic-house stays can vary more than the booking photos suggest.

prac.fltNote: Gjirokastër has no commercial airport. Most travelers route through Tirana, which currently has Gatwick nonstops, then continue several hours south by bus, car, or private transfer; keep destination-level nonstop flags false because the direct service is to Tirana, not Gjirokastër itself.
prac.lang: Albanian is the main language; English is common in tourism-facing stays and cafés, with some Greek and Italian familiarity in the wider south.
prac.visa: Albania's short-stay rules vary by passport, so UK and Irish travelers should check the current Albanian entry terms before travel.
alerts: The old town is steep and cobbled, so rolling luggage and flimsy footwear are a bad combination.
Summer afternoons can feel much hotter than the averages suggest once you start climbing exposed stone streets.
Parking and drop-off are awkward in the historic core, so central guesthouse access often works better with light luggage.

sweet: May to June and September are usually Gjirokastër's best windows for clear light, castle time, and old-town wandering without the harsher midsummer heat.
